titleStmt.title: Letter to Max Green, Chairman of the General Prisons Board, 22 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter was addressed to Max S. Green (1865 - 1922), the chairman of the General Prisons Board. The letter relates to a series of correspondence regarding the collapse of a shared wall between the neighbouring laundry and prison. According to this letter the wall belongs to the laundry and as an addition the author has enclosed a report concerning the rol the prison's ivy played in undermining the wall's structure.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers; CSO RP/1916/2892/2

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
23 NOV 1915
GENERAL
PRISONS BOARD. For Chairman
M
23.11.15 H. M. PRISON
MOUNTJOY 22nd November 1915 With reference to my report of
14th inst. as to wall having been
blown down I beg to further report
that on learning from the Clerk of
Works that there was some doubt
as to whether this wall ever was
built by the Prisons Department
or formed part of their property. I
wrote to the Manager of the N. C. Laundry
as per copy of letter attached. From
letter received in reply (attached) it
will be seen that the Manager thinks
That the wall belongs to the Laundry
& has as stated begun removing the
stones & debris. As regards the suggestion
that the ivy on our side of the wall
was instrumental in causing the
damage I attach report from the
Foreman of Works on this subject.
Pending instructions from the
Board I have not replied to this
letter which also it will be seen
contains a suggestion for opening
negotiations as to the purchase or
renting by the Board of the ground
adjoining wall. Maxwell
Green
The Chairman
General Prisons Board
Dublin Castle
